title: Dubu Jorim Spicy Braised Tofu

seo.title: Dubu Jorim: Easy Spicy Korean Braised Tofu Recipe
seo.metaDescription: Learn how to make authentic Dubu Jorim, a delicious spicy Korean braised tofu dish. This easy vegetarian recipe is packed with flavor and perfect for any meal.
seo.h1: Dubu Jorim: Spicy Korean Braised Tofu Recipe
h2Headings: How to Make Dubu Jorim: Step-by-Step
seo.imageAlt: A plate of freshly made Dubu Jorim, spicy Korean braised tofu, garnished with green onions and sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy this flavorful and easy Dubu Jorim, a classic Korean spicy braised tofu dish that's perfect for a vegetarian meal.

description: Dubu Jorim is a beloved Korean vegetarian dish featuring firm tofu braised in a savory, spicy, and slightly sweet sauce. This comforting meal is packed with umami and a delightful kick, making it a perfect accompaniment to a bowl of warm rice. Its simple yet profound flavors truly showcase the essence of traditional Korean home cooking.

ingredients.notes: pressed and sliced into 1/2-inch thick rectangles
for depth of flavor and spice

instructions.title: Prepare the Tofu
instructions.text: Press the tofu block for at least 30 minutes to remove excess water. You can do this by wrapping it in paper towels, placing it on a plate, and weighing it down with something heavy. Once pressed, slice the tofu into 1/2-inch thick rectangles.

instructions.title: Pan-Fry the Tofu
instructions.text: Heat vegetable oil in a large non-stick pan over medium-high heat. Carefully add the sliced tofu in a single layer, ensuring not to overcrowd the pan. Fry for 3-4 minutes per side, until golden brown and slightly crispy. Remove the fried tofu from the pan and set aside on a plate lined with paper towels to drain excess oil.

instructions.title: Prepare the Braising Sauce
instructions.text: In a small bowl, combine the soy sauce, gochugaru, gochujang, minced garlic, sugar, and sesame oil. Whisk well until all ingredients are thoroughly mixed and the sugar has dissolved. This will be your flavorful braising sauce.

instructions.title: Combine and Simmer
instructions.text: Return the fried tofu to the same pan (no need to clean it). Pour the prepared braising sauce evenly over the tofu. Add the 1/2 cup of water or vegetable broth to the pan. Bring the mixture to a gentle simmer over medium heat.

instructions.title: Braised to Perfection
instructions.text: Once simmering, reduce the heat to medium-low, cover the pan, and let the tofu braise for 15-20 minutes. Periodically, gently spoon the sauce over the tofu to ensure it is thoroughly coated and absorbing the flavors. The sauce should thicken slightly as it reduces.

instructions.title: Garnish and Serve
instructions.text: Once the sauce has thickened to your liking and the tofu is tender and flavorful, remove the pan from the heat. Garnish generously with sliced green onions and toasted sesame seeds. Serve immediately with warm steamed rice for a complete and satisfying Korean vegetarian meal.

tips: Pressing the tofu is crucial for achieving a firm texture and allowing it to absorb the sauce better. Dont skip this step!
Adjust the amount of gochugaru and gochujang to control the spice level according to your preference. For less spice, reduce gochugaru.
Dubu Jorim is best served fresh and warm with a bowl of fluffy white rice. The flavors meld beautifully as it cools slightly.
article:
**Savor the Spice: A Guide to Dubu Jorim, Korean Vegetarian Braised Tofu**

In the vibrant world of Korean cuisine, where flavors dance between savory, sweet, and spicy, there's a beloved dish that stands out for its simplicity, versatility, and incredible taste: Dubu Jorim. This spicy braised tofu is a staple in Korean households, cherished not only for its comforting qualities but also for being a perfectly satisfying and healthy vegetarian option. If you're looking to explore the depths of plant-based Korean cooking, Dubu Jorim is an absolute must-try, offering a delightful blend of textures and an explosion of umami-rich heat that will awaken your taste buds. It’s a dish that proves vegetarian food can be just as exciting and flavorful as any other.

## What is Dubu Jorim?

Dubu Jorim translates quite literally to "braised tofu." At its core, it’s a dish made from firm tofu simmered in a rich, savory, and spicy sauce until the tofu absorbs all the glorious flavors and the sauce thickens to a perfect glaze. Traditionally served as a banchan (a small side dish accompanying a meal), Dubu Jorim is hearty enough to be the star of a vegetarian main course, especially when paired with a bowl of warm rice. The beauty of this dish lies in its humble ingredients—tofu, soy sauce, gochugaru (Korean chili flakes), garlic, and a touch of sweetness—transformed into something truly extraordinary through the magic of braising. It’s a testament to how simple ingredients can create complex and deeply satisfying flavors, making it a favorite for everyday meals and special occasions alike.

## Ingredients for Spicy Braised Tofu

Crafting the perfect Dubu Jorim begins with selecting quality ingredients. Here’s what you’ll need to create this Korean vegetarian delight:

*   **Firm or Extra-Firm Tofu:** This is the star of the show. Opt for firm or extra-firm varieties as they hold their shape best during braising and absorb the sauce beautifully. Avoid silken or soft tofu, which will crumble.
*   **For the Sauce:**
    *   **Gochugaru (Korean Chili Flakes):** Essential for the signature spicy kick and vibrant red color. Adjust the amount to your spice preference.
    *   **Soy Sauce:** Provides the fundamental savory, umami base. Use a good quality Korean or Japanese soy sauce.
    *   **Garlic:** Freshly minced garlic is crucial for aromatic depth.
    *   **Sesame Oil:** Adds a nutty, rich aroma and flavor.
    *   **Sugar or Sweetener:** Balances the heat and saltiness. Brown sugar, white sugar, or even a touch of maple syrup works.
    *   **Water or Vegetable Broth:** The liquid for braising, allowing the tofu to soak up the flavors.
    *   **Optional Gochujang (Korean Chili Paste):** For a deeper, fermented chili flavor and a thicker sauce, though it’s not strictly traditional for Dubu Jorim.
*   **For Garnish:**
    *   **Green Onions (Scallions):** Sliced for freshness and a mild oniony bite.
    *   **Sesame Seeds:** Toasted, for a final flourish and nutty crunch.
*   **Cooking Oil:** A neutral oil like vegetable, canola, or grapeseed oil for pan-frying the tofu (optional but recommended).

## How to Make Dubu Jorim: Step-by-Step

Making Dubu Jorim is a straightforward process that yields incredibly rewarding results. Follow these steps for a delicious outcome:

1.  **Prepare the Tofu:** Begin by pressing your firm tofu. Wrap the block in a few layers of paper towels or a clean kitchen towel, place it on a plate, and put something heavy on top (like a cast-iron pan or a few cans). Let it press for at least 30 minutes to an hour to remove excess water. This step is crucial for achieving a firm texture that won't crumble and will absorb the sauce better. Once pressed, slice the tofu into rectangular pieces, about 1/2 inch thick.
2.  **Pan-Fry the Tofu (Optional but Recommended):** Heat 1-2 tablespoons of cooking oil in a non-stick pan over medium-high heat. Carefully place the tofu slices in a single layer, ensuring not to overcrowd the pan. Fry for 3-5 minutes per side until golden brown and slightly crispy. This creates a lovely crust, adds flavor, and helps the tofu maintain its shape during braising. Remove the tofu from the pan and set aside.
3.  **Prepare the Sauce:** In a small bowl, whisk together all the sauce ingredients: gochugaru, soy sauce, minced garlic, sesame oil, sugar, and water or vegetable broth. Taste and adjust seasonings if necessary – you might want more spice, sweetness, or a dash more soy sauce.
4.  **Braise the Tofu:** In a clean pan or a shallow pot (the same one used for frying is fine, just wipe it clean), arrange the pan-fried tofu slices in a single layer. Pour the prepared sauce evenly over the tofu.
5.  **Simmer and Reduce:** Bring the sauce to a gentle simmer over medium heat. Once simmering, reduce the heat to low, cover the pan, and let it braise for about 10-15 minutes. Gently flip the tofu slices halfway through to ensure even absorption of the sauce.
6.  **Thicken the Sauce:** Remove the lid and continue to simmer on low heat for another 5-10 minutes, or until the sauce has thickened to a syrupy consistency and coats the tofu beautifully. Be careful not to let it dry out completely.
7.  **Garnish and Serve:** Transfer the Dubu Jorim to a serving plate. Garnish generously with sliced green onions and a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds. Serve immediately.

## Tips for Perfect Dubu Jorim

Achieving that perfect balance of flavor and texture in your Dubu Jorim is easier with a few key tips:

*   **Don't Skip Pressing the Tofu:** This is arguably the most important step. Removing excess water prevents watery sauce and allows the tofu to soak up all the delicious flavors.
*   **Fry for Flavor and Texture:** While optional, pan-frying the tofu before braising adds a fantastic depth of flavor and creates a slightly chewy, firm exterior that holds up well.
*   **Adjust to Your Taste:** The beauty of homemade food is customization. Feel free to adjust the amount of gochugaru for more or less spice, and the sugar for your preferred level of sweetness.
*   **Gentle Handling:** Tofu can be delicate, especially when hot. Use a wide, flat spatula to gently flip the tofu slices during braising to prevent them from breaking apart.
*   **Don't Over-Reduce the Sauce:** While you want a thick, glossy sauce, avoid reducing it to the point of burning. It should still be liquid enough to coat the tofu generously.

## Serving Suggestions & Pairings

Dubu Jorim is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in various ways:

*   **Classic Banchan:** Serve it alongside a bowl of freshly steamed rice and other Korean side dishes like kimchi, seasoned spinach, or bean sprouts.
*   **Main Dish:** Enjoy it as the star of your meal with a generous portion of rice.
*   **Bibimbap Topping:** Dice the braised tofu and add it as a flavorful, spicy protein component to your bibimbap bowl.
*   **With Noodles:** Pair it with a simple bowl of plain noodles or cold soba for a hearty meal.
*   **Light Lunch:** Serve chilled Dubu Jorim with a fresh green salad for a light and healthy lunch option.

## Variations: Make it Your Own

Dubu Jorim is wonderfully adaptable, allowing you to experiment with flavors and ingredients:

*   **Add Vegetables:** Introduce thinly sliced zucchini, mushrooms, onions, or carrots to the braising sauce during the last few minutes of cooking for added nutrition and texture.
*   **Umami Boost:** A dash of mushroom powder or a small piece of dried kelp (dashima) simmered in the sauce can enhance the umami flavor profile.
*   **Smokiness:** For a hint of smoky depth, you could add a tiny amount of smoked paprika or a drop of liquid smoke to the sauce.
*   **Sweet and Spicy:** Increase the sugar slightly and consider adding a tablespoon of apple juice or pear juice to the sauce for a more pronounced sweet-and-spicy balance.
*   **Less Spicy:** If you prefer less heat, reduce the gochugaru and consider adding a bit more soy sauce or a touch of a mild chili paste for color without extreme spice.

## Storage & Reheating

Dubu Jorim makes for excellent leftovers. Store any unused portions in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3-4 days. The flavors often deepen and meld even further after a day or two.

To reheat, gently warm the Dubu Jorim on the stovetop over low heat until heated through, adding a splash of water or vegetable broth if the sauce has become too thick. Alternatively, you can microwave it in short intervals, stirring occasionally. Be aware that the tofu might become slightly softer upon reheating, but the flavor will remain superb.

Whether you're a seasoned chef or a curious home cook, diving into the world of Dubu Jorim is a delightful culinary adventure. This spicy braised tofu is more than just a dish; it's a celebration of simple ingredients, bold flavors, and the comforting essence of Korean vegetarian cuisine. Give it a try, and let its savory spice become a new favorite in your kitchen!
